1.15: hos estin eikon tou theou tou aoratou prototokos pases ktiseos (hos estin eikōn tou theou tou aoratou, prōtotokos pasēs ktiseōs): 'who is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation.' The Colossian hymn (1:15-20) is one of the highest Christological statements in the NT. Eikon (image) denotes not a copy of an absent original but the precise visible representation of an invisible reality — Christ is the icon in whom the otherwise-invisible God is seen (cf. John 1:18; Heb 1:3: apaugasma + charakter). Prōtotokos pasēs ktiseōs: 'firstborn of all creation' — prōtotokos is not 'first created' (which would be prōtoktistos) but the one who holds the firstborn's status of priority and authority over all creation; Col 1:16-17 confirms he is the creator, not a creature.
1.19: hoti en auto eudokesen pan to pleroma katoikesai (hoti en autō eudokēsen pan to plērōma katoikēsai): 'for in him all the fullness was pleased to dwell.' Pleroma (fullness) in Gnostic usage (slightly later than Colossians) denotes the totality of divine attributes distributed among aeons. Paul uses the term to assert the counter-claim: the entire divine fullness (not a portion distributed among spiritual intermediaries) resides bodily (somatikos, 2:9) in Christ. The Colossian false teaching apparently proposed a hierarchy of spiritual powers mediating access to God; the hymn's response is that Christ contains the whole without remainder.
1.1: Apostolos Christou Iēsou dia thelēmatos theou (apostle of Christ Jesus through the will of God): the same formula as 1 Cor 1:1 and 2 Cor 1:1, grounding apostolic authority not in self-appointment but in divine commission. Timothy is co-sender (adelphos, brother) though Paul alone is the apostle; Timothy's inclusion signals a shared missionary enterprise and lends the letter communal rather than merely personal authority.
1.2: Colossae was located in the Lykos River valley in southwestern Phrygia (Asia Minor), about 160 km east of Ephesus, near Laodicea and Hierapolis (cf. 4:13-16). Tois hagiois kai pistois adelphois en Christō (to the holy and faithful brothers in Christ): two simultaneous qualifiers — hagiois (holy, set apart by divine calling) and pistois (faithful, trusting). En Christō functions as a locative of sphere: the community exists within the orbit of Christ's lordship.
1.3: Eucharistoumen tō theō patri tou Kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou pantote peri hymōn proseuchomenoi (we give thanks to God the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, always praying for you): Paul's letter openings characteristically include a thanksgiving section (eucharistia) that establishes rapport and anticipates the letter's major themes. 'God the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ' frames the entire letter's theology: the Father-Son relationship is the ground of all Christological claims that follow.
1.4: Akousantes tēn pistin hymōn en Christō Iēsou kai tēn agapēn tēn eis pantas tous hagious (having heard of your faith in Christ Jesus and your love toward all the holy ones): the faith-love pair prepares the way for the third term, hope (v.5), as the grounding reality. Pistis en Christō Iēsou (faith in Christ Jesus) — en can be instrumental (faith through union with Christ) rather than purely directional, signaling participatory rather than merely cognitive trust.
1.5: Dia tēn elpida tēn apokeimenēn hymin en tois ouranois (on account of the hope laid up for you in the heavens): apokeimenēn (laid up/stored up) is a commercial and legal term for goods held in reserve — the hope is a secured deposit. Logos tēs alētheias tou euangeliou (word of truth of the gospel) — a genitive chain: the gospel is defined as the word of truth, implicitly contrasting it with the competing 'philosophy' (2:8) circulating in Colossae.
1.6: Karpophoroun kai auxanomenon (bearing fruit and growing): the gospel is personified as an active agent that produces and expands — the same verbs as Gen 1:28 (be fruitful and multiply), suggesting Paul frames gospel spread as a new-creational fruitfulness. Tēn charin tou theou en alētheia (the grace of God in truth) — the Colossians received the gospel as what it truly is; the phrase anticipates the contrast with a gospel-substitute (1:23: do not move from the hope of the gospel).
1.7: Epaphras is identified as the probable founder of the Colossian church — Paul has not met them personally (2:1). His title — diakonos (minister/servant) and pistos (faithful) — are standard Pauline co-worker designations. Hyper hymōn diakonos tou Christou (minister of Christ on our behalf): the hyper (on behalf of) marks Epaphras as Paul's representative to the Lykos valley; his ministry is apostolically authorized.
1.8: Ho kai dēlōsas hēmin tēn hymōn agapēn en pneumati (who also disclosed to us your love in the Spirit): Epaphras serves as the information channel between Paul and the Colossians. Agapēn en pneumati (love in the Spirit) — en pneumati locates the love within the Spirit's generative activity; community bond is Spirit-sourced, not merely social affinity.
1.9: Epignōsis tou thelēmatos autou en pasē sophia kai synesei pneumatikē (knowledge of his will in all wisdom and spiritual understanding): epignōsis is the intensified form of gnōsis — full or exact knowledge. This may anticipate the false teachers' claim to superior gnōsis (2:23). Sophia (wisdom) and synesis (understanding/insight) are both qualified by pneumatikē — Spirit-given, not philosophically derived.
1.10: Peripatēsai axiōs tou Kyriou eis pasan areskeian (to walk worthily of the Lord unto all pleasing): peripateo (walk) is Paul's standard term for ethical conduct — a Hebraism. Axiōs (worthily) sets the standard: conduct calibrated to the Lord's character. Karpophorountes kai auxanomenoi tē epignōsei tou theou (bearing fruit and growing in the knowledge of God) — the same fruit/growth vocabulary applied to the gospel (v.6) is now applied to the believers themselves.
1.11: Dynamoumenoi en pasē dynamei kata to kratos tēs doxēs autou (being empowered with all power according to the might of his glory): the triple power vocabulary (dynamoumenoi/dynamei/kratos) emphasizes the divine source of endurance. Hypomonē kai makrothymia (endurance and patience): hypomonē is steadfastness under circumstances; makrothymia is patience with persons. Together they cover the full testing-range of community life.
1.12: Hikanōsanti hymas eis tēn merida tou klērou tōn hagiōn en tō phōti (who qualified you for the share of the inheritance of the holy ones in the light): hikanōsanti (qualified/made sufficient) — God is the agent; the believers contribute nothing to their own eligibility. Klērou (inheritance allotment) echoes the OT land-distribution vocabulary (LXX Josh 14-19), now applied eschatologically. Tōn hagiōn (of the holy ones) — probably the angelic assembly of heaven, whose inheritance believers now share by grace.
1.13: Hos erysato hēmas ek tēs exousias tou skotous kai metestēsen eis tēn basileian tou huiou tēs agapēs autou (who rescued us from the authority of darkness and transferred us into the kingdom of the Son of his love): metestēsen (transferred) is a technical Hellenistic administrative term for moving a population from one territory to another — deportation/resettlement vocabulary applied to the cosmic transfer of believers from one dominion to another.
1.14: En hō echomen tēn apolutrōsin, tēn aphesin tōn hamartiōn (in whom we have the redemption, the forgiveness of sins): apolutrōsis (redemption) in its classical sense involves payment of a lytron (ransom price) to release a prisoner or slave. The genitive appositive aphesin tōn hamartiōn (forgiveness of sins) defines the content of redemption, grounding commercial imagery in the relational reality of forgiven guilt. Ephesians 1:7 has an almost identical formula, primary evidence for the Colossians-Ephesians literary relationship.
1.16: En autō ektisthē ta panta (in him all things were created): three prepositions structure vv.16-17 — en autō (in him), di' autou (through him), eis auton (for/toward him). The Son is the sphere, means, and goal of creation. Thronoi, kyriotētes, archai, exousiai (thrones, dominions, rulers, authorities) — the full hierarchy of angelic/cosmic powers; the Son created them all and therefore stands above them all, directly countering any spirit-hierarchy teaching in Colossae.
1.17: Autos estin pro pantōn kai ta panta en autō synestēken (he is before all things and all things hold together in him): pro pantōn implies both temporal priority (pre-existence) and rank priority. Synestēken (hold together) — the perfect tense of synistaō: the present result of a completed act of cohering. The Son is the ontological ground of cosmic coherence; this coheres with the Wisdom tradition (Wis 1:7; Sir 43:26) while grounding cosmic cohesion in a personal agent.
1.18: Autos estin hē kephalē tou sōmatos, tēs ekklēsias (he is the head of the body, the church): in Romans and 1 Corinthians the body metaphor applies to the local congregation; here (and in Ephesians) the 'body' is the universal church whose head is the cosmic Christ. Prōtotokos ek tōn nekrōn (firstborn from the dead) — a second prōtotokos establishes two spheres of supremacy: original creation (v.15) and new creation. Hina genētai en pasin autos prōteuōn (so that he might be in all things pre-eminent) — comprehensive supremacy is the telos of both firstborn-titles.
1.20: Kai di' autou apokatallaxai ta panta eis auton, eirēnopoiēsas dia tou haimatos tou staurou autou (and through him to reconcile all things to himself, making peace through the blood of his cross): apokatallaxai (to reconcile completely) — the apo-prefix on katallassō intensifies toward completeness. The scope 'all things' (ta panta) — both earthly and heavenly — suggests cosmic rather than merely personal reconciliation. Eirēnopoiēsas dia tou haimatos tou staurou — the cross is the historical instrument of cosmic peace, an offense to both Jewish messianic expectation and Roman honor culture.
1.21: Kai hymas pote ontas apēllotriōmenous kai echthrous tē dianoia en tois ergois tois ponērois (and you, once being alienated and enemies in your mind in evil works): apēllotriōmenous (alienated/estranged) — the perfect passive participle describes a completed structural separation; Gentile believers were outside the covenant. Echthrous tē dianoia (enemies in the mind) — enmity located in the cognitive-volitional center, not merely in outward behavior, is a characteristically Pauline anthropological claim.
1.22: Nyni de apokatēllaxen en tō sōmati tēs sarkos autou dia tou thanatou parastēsai hymas hagious kai amōmous kai anegklētous katenōpion autou (but now he has reconciled in the body of his flesh through death, to present you holy and blameless and unaccusable before him): en tō sōmati tēs sarkos autou (in the body of his flesh) — the redundancy emphasizes physical reality of incarnation and death against any docetic reading. The three presentation-adjectives (hagious/amōmous/anegklētous) combine cultic (amōmos = without blemish, LXX sacrificial vocabulary) and forensic (anegklētos = unaccusable) imagery.
1.23: Ei ge epimenetei tē pistei tethemeliōmenoi kai hedraioi kai mē metakinoumenoi apo tēs elpidos tou euangeliou (if indeed you remain in the faith, having been founded and stable and not moved from the hope of the gospel): tethemeliōmenoi (founded) — perfect passive: founding is complete and fixed. Mē metakinoumenoi (not being moved) — a navigational/architectural image of a building being shaken from its foundation. The Colossian teachers are implicitly the destabilizing force. The conditional ei ge is motivational, not expressing doubt about the outcome.
1.24: Antanaplērō ta hysterēmata tōn thlipseōn tou Christou en tē sarki mou hyper tou sōmatos autou, ho estin hē ekklēsia (I fill up what is lacking of the afflictions of Christ in my flesh for the sake of his body, which is the church): hysterēmata tōn thlipseōn tou Christou (what is lacking of the afflictions of Christ) does not imply that Christ's atonement is incomplete. Rather, the messianic woes — the eschatological tribulations that precede the end (Dan 12:1; Mark 13:19-20) — have an appointed measure; Paul's apostolic sufferings fill that eschatological quota for the church's sake.
1.25: Kata tēn oikonomian tou theou tēn dotheisan moi eis hymas plērōsai ton logon tou theou (according to the stewardship of God given to me toward you, to fulfill/complete the word of God): oikonomia (stewardship/administration) — household-management vocabulary applied to Paul's divinely assigned role in the economy of salvation. Plērōsai ton logon tou theou (to complete the word of God) — bringing the word to its intended reach (the Gentiles), not fulfilling a prophecy.
1.26: To mystērion to apokekrymmenon apo tōn aiōnōn kai apo tōn geneōn, nyn de ephanerōthē tois hagiois autou (the mystery hidden from the ages and from the generations, but now revealed to his holy ones): mystērion in Paul consistently means a formerly hidden divine purpose now publicly disclosed — not an esoteric secret. Apo tōn aiōnōn kai apo tōn geneōn — two measures of hiddenness: cosmological (aiōnes) and historical (geneai). Nyn de (but now) — the eschatological now of disclosure.
1.27: Christos en hymin, hē elpis tēs doxēs (Christ in you, the hope of glory): the revealed content of the mystery. En hymin (in you/among you) — the indwelling of Christ in Gentile believers is the mystery's specific content: God's presence, once tied to temple and Israel, now inhabits Gentile bodies. Hē elpis tēs doxēs (the hope that is/consists of glory) — the genitive of content: the eschatological glory to be revealed at Christ's Parousia.
1.28: Panta anthrōpon teleion en Christō (every person mature in Christ): the triple panta anthrōpon (every person) is rhetorically emphatic — the gospel is universal, not esoteric. Teleion en Christō (mature/complete in Christ) — teleios is the goal of Christian formation; the false teachers may have promised a higher teleiotēs through their practices. Paul's response: genuine maturity is in Christ alone, not in supplemental spiritual programs.
1.29: Kopiō agōnizomenos kata tēn energeian autou tēn energoumenēn en emoi en dynamei (I labor, striving according to his working that works in me in power): kopiō (labor, toil) describes demanding physical and mental work; agōnizomenos (striving, contending) is athletic/military contest vocabulary. The paradox of apostolic ministry: the effort is real, yet the energy source is divine. Energeia energoumenē (working being-worked) — a cognate construction emphasizing that divine operation is the ground of all human apostolic labor.

2.14: exaleipsas to kath' hemon cheirographon tois dogmasin ho en hypenantios hemin (exaleipsas to kath' hēmōn cheirographon tois dogmasin ho ēn hypenantios hēmin): 'having canceled the record of debt (cheirographon) that stood against us with its legal demands.' Cheirographon (handwritten document/bond) was a legal IOU — a debtor's acknowledgment of what was owed. The metaphor: humanity's accumulated debt of sin-obligations constituted a document that indicted us. proseilosas auto to staurō: 'nailing it to the cross.' The practice of affixing a notice of charges (titulus) to the cross of a condemned person (John 19:19-22: 'Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews') provides the image: our indicting debt-document was nailed to the cross with Christ, absorbing its condemnation in his death.
2.15: apekdysamenos tas archas kai tas exousias edeigmatisen en parresia thriambeusas autous en auto (apekdysamenos tas archas kai tas exousias edeigmatisen en parrēsia, thriambeusas autous en autō): 'disarming the rulers and authorities and putting them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him.' The cosmic powers (archai kai exousiai) that featured in the Colossian false teaching as spiritual intermediaries are publicly stripped of their authority by the cross. The triumphal procession image: in a Roman triumph, defeated enemies were displayed as captives behind the general's chariot. Christ's cross is the paradoxical triumph: precisely in what looks like defeat, the powers are disarmed and publicly humiliated.
2.1: Hēlikon agōna echō hyper hymōn kai tōn en Laodikeia kai hosoi ouch heōrakasin to prosōpon mou en sarki (how great a struggle I have for you and for those in Laodicea and however many have not seen my face in the flesh): agōna (struggle/contest) is the athletic and military contest term used for prayerful contending (cf. 4:12: Epaphras wrestling in prayer). Paul's concern for communities he has not personally visited is distinctive — he acts as a universal apostle, not merely a local pastor.
2.2: Hina paraklēthōsin hai kardiai autōn, symbibasthentas en agapē kai eis panta ploutos tēs plērophorias tēs syneseōs, eis epignōsin tou mystēriou tou theou, Christou (so that their hearts may be encouraged, being knit together in love and into all the wealth of full assurance of understanding, into knowledge of the mystery of God, which is Christ): symbibasthentas en agapē (knit together in love) — symbibazo is used in 2:19 of the body being joined; here the community is the body being knit. Plērophorias (full assurance/certainty) contrasts with the speculative uncertainty the false teachers may have promoted.
2.3: En hō eisin pantes hoi thēsauroi tēs sophias kai gnōseōs apokryphoi (in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge): thēsauroi (treasures) — the full deposit of wisdom is concentrated in Christ. Apokryphoi (hidden) — a wordplay: the mystery once hidden from the ages (1:26) is now revealed in Christ, yet wisdom remains 'hidden' in him in the sense that only union with Christ unlocks it. This preempts any claim to additional esoteric wisdom beyond Christ.
2.4: Hina mēdeis hymas paralogizētai en pithanologia (so that no one may reason you astray by persuasive argument): paralogizētai (reason astray) — para- prefix signals deviation from correct reasoning. Pithanologia (persuasive speech) is a rhetoric term; the false teachers' arguments are outwardly compelling but internally specious. The pastoral goal is to inoculate the community against subtle intellectual seduction.
2.5: Tēn taxin kai to stereōma tēs eis Christon pisteōs hymōn (your order and the firmness of your faith in Christ): taxin (order/arrangement) is a military term for ranks in formation; stereōma (firmness/solid foundation) is either the un-breached state of a formation or the fortress wall resisting assault. Paul's military metaphor frames the Colossians as a community holding a position against the errorists' attack.
2.6: Hōs oun parelabete ton Christon Iēsoun ton Kyrion, en autō peripateitei (just as you received Christ Jesus the Lord, walk in him): parelabete (received) — the technical term for receiving authoritative tradition (paradosis); the same verb as 1 Cor 11:23 (receiving the Lord's Supper tradition). The implication: the teaching they received from Epaphras is the authoritative tradition; any supplementation is deviation. Peripateitei (walk) — ethical conduct shaped by the received gospel.
2.7: Errizōmenoi kai epoikodomoumeoi en autō kai bebaioumenoi tē pistei kathōs edidachthēte, perisseuontes en [tē] eucharistia (having been rooted and being built up in him and confirmed in the faith as you were taught, overflowing in thanksgiving): the mixed metaphor of root (agricultural, perfect: already fixed) and building (architectural, present: ongoing) is characteristic Paul (cf. 1 Cor 3:9-15). Perisseuontes en tē eucharistia (overflowing in thanksgiving) — the community's surplus response to grace received.
2.8: Sylagōgōn dia tēs philosophias kai kenēs apatēs kata tēn paradosin tōn anthrōpōn, kata ta stoicheia tou kosmou kai ou kata Christon (plundering you through philosophy and empty deceit according to human tradition, according to the elemental powers of the world and not according to Christ): stoicheia tou kosmou (elemental powers of the world) — most coherent in context (2:20: died with Christ to the stoicheia; 2:15: cosmic powers disarmed) as spiritual forces governing the cosmic order, to whose regulations the false teachers' calendar and dietary system is tied. Ou kata Christon (not according to Christ) — the decisive criterion: any teaching not measured by Christ is empty deceit.
2.9: Hoti en autō katoikei pan to plērōma tēs theotētos sōmatikōs (because in him dwells all the fullness of the Godhead bodily): theotētos (of the Godhead/Deity) is the abstract noun of divinity itself — distinct from theiotētos (Godlike quality, Rom 1:20) which denotes divine characteristics. Theotētos means the full ontological being of God. Sōmatikōs (bodily) counters any distribution of the fullness among angelic intermediaries: all divine fullness is concentrated in the incarnate body of Christ.
2.10: Kai este en autō peplērōmenoi, hos estin hē kephalē pasēs archēs kai exousias (and you are in him having been made full, who is the head of every ruler and authority): peplērōmenoi (having been filled/made complete) — perfect passive: the filling is complete and its effects remain. The Colossians already have the fullness that is in Christ; no additional spiritual supplement is needed. Kephalē pasēs archēs kai exousias (head of every ruler and authority) — Christ's headship over the cosmic powers renders their independent veneration unnecessary.
2.11: Peritomē acheiropoiētō en tē apekdysei tou sōmatos tēs sarkos, en tē peritomē tou Christou (circumcision not made with hands in the stripping off of the body of flesh, in the circumcision of Christ): acheiropoiētos (not made with hands) — Paul's contrast between physical ritual and its eschatological fulfillment (cf. 2 Cor 5:1; Heb 9:11). Apekdysis tou sōmatos tēs sarkos (stripping off the body of flesh) — the circumcision metaphor becomes a clothing/unclothing metaphor; the deep circumcision is union with Christ's death.
2.12: Syntaphentes autō en tō baptismati, en hō kai synēgerthēte dia tēs pisteōs tēs energeias tou theou tou egeirantos auton ek tōn nekrōn (having been buried with him in baptism, in which you were also raised with him through faith in the working of God who raised him from the dead): baptism as death and resurrection — Romans 6's participatory logic applied here. Notably, in Colossians the resurrection is stated in the past tense (synēgerthēte), a more realized eschatology than Rom 6:5 ('we will be united' — future), suggesting a developed Christological framing of baptism.
2.13: Kai hymas nekrous ontas [en] tois paraptōmasin kai tē akrobystia tēs sarkos hymōn, synezōopoiēsen hymas syn autō, charissamenos hēmin panta ta paraptōmata (you, being dead in your transgressions and the uncircumcision of your flesh, he made alive together with him, having forgiven all the transgressions): nekrous ontas en tois paraptōmasin (being dead in transgressions) — Eph 2:1 has nearly identical language. Charissamenos (having forgiven/graciously given) — from charizomai, cognate with charis (grace): the forgiveness is a gracious gift.
2.16: Mē oun tis hymas krinetō en brōsei kai en posei ē en merei heortēs ē neomēnias ē sabbatōn (therefore let no one judge you in eating and drinking or in respect to a feast or new moon or Sabbaths): food/drink restrictions and the Jewish calendar (annual feasts, monthly new moons, weekly Sabbaths) indicate a Jewish or Jewish-syncretic element in the Colossian teaching. The particular prohibitions suggest a system of dietary purity tied to the observance of cosmic-calendar time-units under angelic governance.
2.17: Ha estin skia tōn mellontōn, to de sōma tou Christou (which are a shadow of the things to come, but the body is of Christ): skia/sōma (shadow/body) is a Platonic-Hellenistic distinction between the shadowy copy and the real substance, adapted for Christological typology. The OT institutions are the shadow cast by the coming reality; Christ is the substance (sōma) that casts the shadow. This is a positive typological reading, not mere abrogation.
2.18: Katabrabeuetō thelōn en tapeinophrosunē kai thrēskeia tōn angelōn, ha heōraken embateuōn, eikē physiōmenos hypo tou noos tēs sarkos autou (let no one disqualify you, delighting in humility and worship of angels, entering into what he has seen, being inflated in vain by the mind of the flesh): katabrabeuetō (disqualify as umpire/judge) — the false teacher acts as an unauthorized umpire. Thrēskeia tōn angelōn (worship of angels) — whether angels are worshipped or the teachers are performing angel-like worship, the common element is veneration of angelic powers. Embateuōn — used in mystery religion contexts for entering sacred space after initiation, suggesting visionary or mystical access claims.
2.19: Kai ou kratōn tēn kephalēn, ex hou pan to sōma dia tōn haphōn kai syndesmōn epichorēgoumenon kai symbibazomenon auxei tēn auxēsin tou theou (not holding to the head, from whom the whole body, supplied and knit together through the joints and ligaments, grows with God's growth): the false teacher has severed connection with Christ the head. The anatomical body-image: haphōn kai syndesmōn (joints and ligaments) hold the body together. The church grows only by maintaining connection to Christ — all other spiritual programs bypass the head and disrupt organic growth.
2.20: Ei apethanete syn Christō apo tōn stoicheiōn tou kosmou, ti hōs zōntes en kosmō dogmatizesthe (if you died with Christ from the elemental powers of the world, why, as though living in the world, do you submit to regulations): co-death with Christ (cf. 2:12; 3:3; Rom 6:2-7) breaks legal obligation to the old powers — death dissolves contractual obligation (cf. Rom 7:1-4). Dogmatizesthe (you submit to decrees) — the cognitive power of the stoicheia's regulatory system is challenged: you have died to it; why live as though still under it?
2.21: Mē hapsē mēde geusē mēde thigēs (do not handle, do not taste, do not touch): Paul quotes the errorists' regulations in compressed form — three prohibitions of tactile/gustatory contact in ascending restriction. The triple prohibition caricatures the dietary and purity regime by presenting it in its bare regulatory form, divorced from any theological justification.
2.22: Ha estin panta eis phthoran tē apochrēsei, kata ta entalmata kai didaskalias tōn anthrōpōn (which are all things unto corruption in use, according to human commandments and teachings): eis phthoran tē apochrēsei — the prohibited things are perishable: food decays on digestion. Paul echoes Mark 7:19 (food cannot defile) and Jesus' contrast between internal and external defilement. Entalmata kai didaskalias tōn anthrōpōn (commandments and teachings of humans) — Isa 29:13 LXX (cited in Mark 7:7): the errorists' regulations have the status of the human traditions Jesus condemned.
2.23: Hatina estin logon men echonta sophias en ethelothrēskia kai tapeinophrosunē kai apheidia sōmatos, ouk en timē tini pros plēsmonēn tēs sarkos (which have a reputation of wisdom in self-imposed worship and humility and severity to the body, not in any honor against the satisfaction of the flesh): ethelothrēskia (self-imposed worship) — a hapax; a compound of voluntary will (thelō) and worship (thrēskeia). Apheidia sōmatos (harsh treatment of the body) — impressive asceticism. Paul's verdict: it has the appearance of wisdom but no actual power against fleshly indulgence — the very problem it claims to solve, it does not solve.

3.3: apethanete gar kai he zoe hymon kekryptai syn to Christo en to theo (apethanete gar kai hē zōē hymōn kekryptai syn tō Christō en tō theō): 'for you have died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God.' The perfect passive kekryptai (is and remains hidden) describes the present state: the believer's true life is concealed within the divine-Christological reality, not visible to external observation. This 'hiddenness' will be resolved at the parousia (v. 4: 'when Christ who is your life appears, you also will appear with him in glory') — eschatological glory is the unveiling of what is now hidden. The mystical union language ('with Christ in God') positions the believer's ontological security within the relationship between the Son and the Father.
3.1: Ei oun synēgerthēte tō Christō, ta anō zēteite, hou ho Christos estin en dexia tou theou kathēmenos (if then you were raised with Christ, seek the things above, where Christ is, seated at the right hand of God): co-resurrection with Christ creates a new orientation. Ta anō (things above) is not Platonic otherworldliness but eschatological priority — what matters in the light of Christ's reign. En dexia tou theou kathēmenos (seated at the right hand of God) — Ps 110:1, the most-cited OT text in the NT; Christ's session frames all cosmic-rule claims in the letter.
3.2: Ta anō phroneite, mē ta epi tēs gēs (set your minds on things above, not on earthly things): phroneite (set your minds/think) — the verb of cognitive and volitional orientation; the same root as phronēsis (practical wisdom). The contrast 'above/earthly' is eschatological, not ontological: what is above is the realm of the ascended Christ; what is earthly is the passing age's value-system.
3.4: Hotan ho Christos phanerōthē, hē zōē hymōn, tote kai hymeis syn autō phanerōthēsesthe en doxē (when Christ, your life, is revealed, then you also will be revealed with him in glory): the Parousia as disclosure (phanerōsis) of what is now hidden. Hē zōē hymōn (your life) — Christ is identified with the believer's life, a participatory depth comparable to John 14:6 ('I am the life'). Eschatological glory is the unveiling of what is now hidden in Christ.
3.5: Nekrōsate oun ta melē ta epi tēs gēs: porneian, akatharsia, pathos, epithumian kakēn, kai tēn pleonexian hētis estin eidōlolatria (put to death your members that are on the earth: sexual immorality, impurity, passion, evil desire, and greed which is idolatry): the imperative nekrōsate (put to death) follows from the indicative of co-death with Christ (2:20; 3:3). Five vices culminating in pleonexia identified as idolatry — the worship of having over worship of God. The sequence moves from outward acts to inward desires to the root disposition.
3.6: Di' ha erchetai hē orgē tou theou (because of these the wrath of God is coming): orgē tou theou (wrath of God) — in Paul, not arbitrary anger but the structured divine consequence of suppressing truth and choosing creature over Creator (Rom 1:18-32). The present tense erchetai signals the certainty of future judgment: the vices are not merely socially harmful but cosmically accountable.
3.7: En hois kai hymeis periepatesate pote, hote ezēte en toutois (in which also you once walked, when you were living in these things): the past tense (periepatesate/ezēte) frames pre-conversion life as genuinely past. The change is ontological (1:13: transferred from one dominion to another) rather than merely moral. The vice list is not theoretical but autobiographical for Gentile readers who lived within these patterns.
3.8: Nyni de apothesthe kai hymeis ta panta: orgēn, thymon, kakian, blasphēmian, aischrologian ek tou stomatos hymōn (but now also put off all these things: anger, rage, malice, slander, obscene speech from your mouth): a second vice list, now relational. Orgē (settled anger), thymos (passionate outburst), kakia (ill-will), blasphēmia (defamatory speech), aischrologia (obscene speech). The progression maps inner states (orgē, thymos, kakia) to their verbal expression (blasphēmia, aischrologia).
3.9: Mē pseudesthe eis allēlous, apekdysamenoi ton palaion anthrōpon syn tais praxesin autou (do not lie to each other, having stripped off the old man with his practices): apekdysamenoi (having stripped off) — the aorist participle refers to the once-for-all act at conversion/baptism; the imperative builds on it. Palaios anthrōpos (old man) — the pre-conversion self as an entire mode of existence, not merely bad habits. The clothing metaphor (strip/put on) continues through v.14.
3.10: Kai endysamenoi ton neon ton anakainoumenon eis epignōsin kat' eikona tou ktisantos auton (and having put on the new, which is being renewed unto knowledge according to the image of the one who created it): anakainoumenon (being renewed) — present passive: ongoing renewal. Kat' eikona tou ktisantos auton (according to the image of the one who created it) — the new creation restores the imago Dei (Gen 1:26-27), distorted at the Fall. Christ as the image of God (1:15) is the template; the believer is progressively re-imaged toward the Creator's original intention.
3.11: Hopou ouk eni Hellēn kai Ioudaios, peritomē kai akrobystia, barbaros, Skythēs, doulos, eleutheros, alla panta kai en pasin Christos (where there is neither Greek nor Jew, circumcised or uncircumcised, barbarian, Scythian, slave or free, but Christ is all and in all): the new humanity transcends the three primary social binaries: ethnic (Greek/Jew), religio-cultural (circumcised/uncircumcised), and social-economic (slave/free). The Scythian — in Greco-Roman ethnography the nadir of civilization — is explicitly included. Panta kai en pasin Christos (Christ is all and in all) — the most comprehensive inclusion formula in Paul.
3.12: Endysasthe oun, hōs eklektoi tou theou hagioi kai ēgapēmenoi, splanchna oiktirmou, chrēstotēta, tapeinophrosunēn, prautēta, makrothymian (put on, as chosen ones of God, holy and beloved, heart of compassion, kindness, humble-mindedness, gentleness, long-suffering): the virtue list is structured by communal identity (eklektoi, hagioi, ēgapēmenoi — election vocabulary from Deuteronomy and the Psalms). Splanchna oiktirmou (heart of compassion) — splanchna (viscera) is the seat of deep gut-level emotion in Hellenistic and Jewish anthropology.
3.13: Charizomenoi heautois, ean tis pros tina echē momphēn: kathōs kai ho Kyrios echarisato hymin, houtōs kai hymeis (forgiving one another, if anyone has a complaint against someone: just as the Lord forgave you, so also you): charizomenoi (forgiving/graciously giving) — from charis; to forgive is to extend grace. The Christological standard: the Lord's prior forgiveness of the believers establishes the mode of their forgiveness of each other — not contractual reciprocity but grace-precedent.
3.14: Epi pasi de toutois tēn agapēn, ho estin syndesmos tēs teleiotētos (and over all these things love, which is the bond of completeness): syndesmos tēs teleiotētos (bond of completeness/perfection) — agapē is the integrating virtue that holds all other virtues together, as a belt holds a garment. Teleiotētos (completeness, maturity) connects to the letter's goal of presenting everyone teleios (1:28); love is what completes the virtue structure.
3.15: Kai hē eirēnē tou Christou brabeuetō en tais kardiais hymōn, eis hēn kai eklēthēte en heni sōmati: kai eucharistoi ginesthe (and let the peace of Christ umpire in your hearts, to which also you were called in one body: and become thankful): brabeuetō (umpire/decide) — the athletic umpire who adjudicates disputed contests; the peace of Christ settles community conflicts. Eucharistoi ginesthe (become thankful) — the imperative of becoming: an ongoing cultivation of habitual gratitude.
3.16: Ho logos tou Christou enoikeito en hymin plousiōs en pasē sophia didaskontes kai nouthetountes heautous psalmois hymnois ōdais pneumatikais en [tē] chariti adontes en [tais] kardiais hymōn tō theō (let the word of Christ dwell in you richly in all wisdom, teaching and admonishing one another with psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs, singing in grace in your hearts to God): enoikeito en hymin plousiōs (dwell in you richly) — enoikeō (dwell within); the word takes up permanent residence in the community. The three song-types (psalmos, hymnos, ōdē pneumatikē) suggest the full range of the community's corporate worship as the primary medium of word-formation.
3.17: Kai pan ho ti ean poiēte en logō ē en ergō, panta en onomati Kyriou Iēsou, eucharistountes tō theō patri di' autou (and whatever you do, in word or deed, all in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through him): en onomati Kyriou Iēsou (in the name of the Lord Jesus) — to act in Christ's name is to act as his representative under his lordship. This verse sets the theological principle governing the household code (3:18–4:1): the Kyrios who is the standard for all conduct is Christ, not Caesar or social convention.
3.18: Hai gynaikes, hypotassesthe tois andrasin hymōn, hōs anēken en Kyriō (wives, submit yourselves to your own husbands, as is fitting in the Lord): the haustafeln (household code) adapts the Hellenistic household management schema (Aristotle's Politics 1.5) with Christological qualifiers. Hypotassesthe — middle/reflexive voice: voluntary ordering of oneself within a relational structure. Hōs anēken en Kyriō (as is fitting in the Lord) — Christ's lordship both frames and potentially limits any conventional household obligation.
3.19: Hoi andres, agapaite tas gynaikas kai mē pikrainesthe pros autas (husbands, love your wives and do not be harsh/bitter toward them): the husband's obligation is active love (agapaite), not the exercise of legal authority. The prohibition of pikrainesthe (bitterness/harshness — pikros = sharp, bitter) restricts the exercise of power that would make the relationship toxic. The household code within the Christ-frame reshapes power dynamics toward mutual service.
3.20: Ta tekna, hypakouete tois goneusin kata panta, touto gar euareston estin en Kyriō (children, obey your parents in all things, for this is pleasing in the Lord): hypakouete (obey) is stronger than hypotassesthe (submit) — children obey, wives voluntarily submit. Euareston estin en Kyriō (is pleasing in the Lord) — the standard is the Lord's pleasure, not merely social convention, placing a Christological qualifier on the conventional expectation.
3.21: Hoi pateres, mē erethizete ta tekna hymōn, hina mē athumōsin (fathers, do not provoke/exasperate your children, so that they will not become discouraged): in the Greco-Roman patria potestas system, the father had absolute legal authority over children including life and death. Paul's counter-injunction limits paternal power by the child's interior state: fathering that produces athumia (despondency/loss of spirit) violates the lordship of Christ. This directly restricts ancient paternal authority.
3.22: Hoi douloi, hypakouete kata panta tois kata sarka kyriois, mē en ophthalmodouleia hōs anthrōpareskoi, all' en haplotēti kardias phoboumenoi ton Kyrion (slaves, obey your earthly masters in all things, not in eye-service as people-pleasers, but in singleness of heart fearing the Lord): ophthalmodouleia (eye-service) — Paul may have coined this compound; service performed only when watched. En haplotēti kardias (in singleness/simplicity of heart) — undivided, wholehearted engagement. The ultimate accountability is to the divine Kyrios, not the human master.
3.23: Ho ean poiēte, ek psychēs ergazesthe, hōs tō Kyriō kai ouk anthrōpois (whatever you do, work from the soul, as to the Lord and not to humans): ek psychēs (from the soul/whole being) — not reluctant compliance but wholehearted engagement. The reorientation of the work motive to the divine Kyrios transforms the experience of coerced slave labor by relocating the ultimate accountability relationship — a profound existential shift within an unjust social system.
3.24: Apolēmpsesthe tēn antapodosis tēs klēronomiās: tō Kyriō Christō douleuete (you will receive the repayment of the inheritance: you are serving the Lord Christ): antapodosis tēs klēronomiās (repayment of the inheritance) — a paradox: inheritance is received by birthright, not earned; 'repayment' is merited. The combination destabilizes both categories: the eschatological inheritance is guaranteed by grace but distributed in correspondence to faithful service. The slave's true Kyrios is not the human master but Christ the divine Lord.
3.25: Ho gar adikōn komisetai ho ēdikēsen, kai ouk estin prosōpolēmpsia (for the one who does wrong will receive back what wrong he did, and there is no partiality): komisetai ho ēdikēsen (will receive back the wrong done) — exact correspondence of wrong and consequence. Prosōpolēmpsia (partiality/face-receiving) — literally 'receiving the face' as a Semitic idiom for status-based favoritism. God's judgment is impartial: slave and master face the same standard. A quiet but radical social leveler within the household code.

4.1: Hoi kyrioi, to dikaion kai tēn isotēta tois doulois parechesthe, eidotes hoti kai hymeis kyrion echete en ouranō (masters, provide what is right and fair to your slaves, knowing that you also have a Master in heaven): to dikaion kai tēn isotēta (what is right and equality/fairness) — isotēs (equality, proportional fairness) is the Aristotelian term for proportional justice. The theological grounding — 'you also have a Master in heaven' — renders every earthly master a slave of the divine Kyrios, subject to the same standard.
4.2: Tē proseuchē proskartereitei, grēgorountes en autē en eucharistia (persist in prayer, being watchful in it with thanksgiving): proskartereitei (persist/continue steadfastly) — the same verb used in Acts 1:14; 2:42; Rom 12:12 for devoted, sustained prayer. Grēgorountes (being watchful/vigilant) — eschatological watchfulness vocabulary (Mark 13:35-37; 1 Cor 16:13; 1 Thess 5:6). Prayer is not relaxed devotion but alert engagement.
4.3: Hina ho theos anoixē hēmin thyran tou logou lalēsai to mystērion tou Christou, di' ho kai dedemai (that God may open for us a door of the word to speak the mystery of Christ, for which I am also bound): thyran tou logou (door of the word) — Paul uses the 'open door' idiom in 1 Cor 16:9 and 2 Cor 2:12 for missionary opportunity. The request is not for personal release but for continued proclamation. Di' ho kai dedemai (for which I am also in chains) — the mystery of Christ (Gentile inclusion) is specifically why Paul is imprisoned; the chains validate the message.
4.4: Hina phanerōsō auto hōs dei me lalēsai (so that I may reveal it as it is necessary for me to speak): phanerōsō (reveal/make manifest) — the same verb used for the revelation of the hidden mystery (1:26). Paul's proclamation is itself a form of the mystery's revelation. Hōs dei me lalēsai (as it is necessary for me to speak) — the dei (it is necessary/must) expresses divine compulsion; proclamation is not optional (cf. 1 Cor 9:16: necessity is laid upon me).
4.5: En sophia peripateitei pros tous exō, ton kairon exagorazomenoi (walk in wisdom toward those outside, redeeming/buying up the opportune time): pros tous exō (toward those outside) — outsiders to the community, whose evaluation of the community's conduct is real and consequential. Ton kairon exagorazomenoi (buying up the opportune time) — exagorazō applied to kairos (opportune moment): the image of a merchant seizing goods when available. Each encounter with outsiders is a kairos to be seized or lost.
4.6: Ho logos hymōn pantote en chariti, hērtymenos halati, eidenai pōs dei hymas heni ekastō apokrineshai (your speech always in grace, seasoned with salt, to know how you ought to answer each one): en chariti hērtymenos halati (seasoned with salt) — salt as seasoning and preservative was central to ancient hospitality and cuisine; the metaphor implies speech that is flavorful, fitting, and preserving rather than insipid or flattering. Heni ekastō apokrineshai (to answer each individual one) — contextual sensitivity: wisdom in speech is responsive to the specific person, not formulaic.
4.7: Tychikos ho agapētos adelphos kai pistos diakonos kai syndoulos en Kyriō (Tychicus, the beloved brother and faithful minister and fellow slave in the Lord): Tychicus appears in Acts 20:4; Eph 6:21-22 (nearly identical commendation, suggesting Col and Eph were carried simultaneously); 2 Tim 4:12; Tit 3:12. The three-fold title marks him as a trusted representative who carries the letter and supplements it orally — standard ancient letter-carrier function.
4.8: Hina gnōte ta peri hēmōn kai parakalese tas kardias hymōn (that you may know the things concerning us and that he may encourage your hearts): the letter-carrier's dual function in ancient epistolary practice: (1) carry the written text; (2) supplement it with oral information and embody the sender's presence. Parakalese tas kardias hymōn (encourage your hearts) — Paraclete/comfort vocabulary; Tychicus brings pastoral encouragement in person, not just information.
4.9: Syn Onēsimō tō pistō kai agapētō adelphō, hos estin ex hymōn (with Onesimus the faithful and beloved brother, who is one of you): Onesimus is the runaway slave at the center of the letter to Philemon. His return to Colossae accompanies both letters simultaneously. The description 'faithful and beloved brother' — identical language to Tychicus — affirms his equal standing in Christ; this is preparation for the community's reception of Philemon's returned slave as a brother.
4.10: Aristarchus ho synaichmalōtos mou, kai Markos ho anepsios Barnaba (Aristarchus my fellow prisoner, and Mark the cousin of Barnabas): Aristarchus appears in Acts 19:29; 20:4; 27:2 (sailed with Paul to Rome). Mark is John Mark of Acts 12-15; his earlier failure caused the Paul-Barnabas split (Acts 15:37-39); he is now rehabilitated as a valued co-worker (cf. 2 Tim 4:11: 'Mark is useful to me for ministry'). Anepsios (cousin) — a precise kinship term, rare in the NT.
4.11:
Kai Iēsous ho legomenos Ioustos... houtoi monoi synergo
i eis tēn basileian tou theou hoitines egenēthēsan moi parēgoria (and Jesus called Justus... these alone are my fellow workers for the kingdom of God, who have become a comfort to me): Jesus Justus — pairing the Jewish name (Joshua) with the Latin cognomen (Justus = righteous) is documented in papyri. The melancholy 'these alone' among circumcised believers suggests that most Jewish Christians have withdrawn from or opposed Paul's Gentile mission. Parēgoria (comfort, consolation) — a medical term for soothing medicine; a hapax legomenon in the NT.
4.12: Epaphras ho ex hymōn, doulos Christou Iēsou, pantote agōnizomenos hyper hymōn en tais proseuchais, hina stathēte teleoi kai peplērōmenoi en panti thelēmati tou theou (Epaphras who is one of you, a slave of Christ Jesus, always contending for you in prayers, that you may stand mature and fully assured in all the will of God): agōnizomenos (contending/wrestling) — the athletic contest term for intense prayer (cf. 1:29: Paul's apostolic labor). Epaphras' prayer content mirrors Paul's proclaimed purpose (1:28: present everyone teleios): the pastor prays for what the apostle proclaims.
4.13: Martyrō gar autō hoti echei polyn ponon hyper hymōn kai tōn en Laodikeia kai tōn en Hierapolei (for I testify for him that he has great toil for you and for those in Laodicea and those in Hierapolis): martyrō (I testify/bear witness) — Paul's endorsement functions as a character reference; Epaphras cannot testify for himself. The Lykos valley trio (Colossae, Laodicea, Hierapolis) form Epaphras' pastoral field. Polyn ponon (great toil/pain) — costly, labor-intensive engagement.
4.14: Loukas ho iatros ho agapētos kai Dēmas (Luke the beloved physician and Demas): Luke the physician — the same Luke associated with the 'we' sections of Acts (16:10-17; 20:5-15; 27:1-28:16) and traditionally credited with Luke-Acts. The medical designation iatros (physician) is the primary evidence for the tradition that Luke was a doctor. Demas appears in Phlm 24 as a co-worker and in 2 Tim 4:10 with the tragic note: 'having loved the present age, he deserted me.' The bare listing here — no commendation — may already signal ambivalence.
4.15: Aspasasthe tous en Laodikeia adelphous kai Nymphan kai tēn kat' oikon autēs ekklēsian (greet the brothers in Laodicea and Nympha and the church in her house): Nymphan — the name is ambiguous between masculine and feminine; textual variants with autou/autēs/autōn reflect ancient scribal uncertainty about gender. The feminine reading has good manuscript support. If Nympha, she is a woman house-church host in Laodicea — consistent with the pattern of Lydia (Acts 16:15) and Mary (Acts 12:12) hosting early churches.
4.16: Hotan anagnōsthē par' hymin hē epistolē, poiēsate hina kai en tē Laodikeon ekklēsia anagnōsthē, kai tēn ek Laodikias hina kai hymeis anagnōte (when the letter is read among you, see that it is also read in the church of the Laodiceans, and that you in turn read the letter from Laodicea): the circular letter system — Pauline letters shared between communities. The 'letter from Laodicea' is most widely identified with Ephesians (which lacks a specific local address in the best manuscripts, suggesting a circular letter), or a now-lost letter. The instruction shows Paul's letters were community property, not private correspondence.
4.17: Kai eipate Archippō: blepei tēn diakonian hēn parelabete en Kyriō, hina autēn plērois (and say to Archippus: see to the ministry which you received in the Lord, so that you may fulfill it): Archippus appears in Phlm 2 as a 'fellow soldier' (implying active service). The public exhortation delivered through a letter read aloud to the congregation is a form of community accountability. Plērois (fulfill/complete) — the same verb as Paul's own commission (1:25: to complete the word of God); every ministry-holder has a plērōsis to accomplish.
4.18: Ho aspasmōs tē emē cheiri Paulou. mnēmoneuete mou tōn desmōn. hē charis meth' hymōn (the greeting is in my own hand — Paul's. Remember my chains. Grace be with you): the autograph closing authenticates the letter against forgeries (cf. Gal 6:11; 1 Cor 16:21; 2 Thess 3:17). Mnēmoneuete mou tōn desmōn (remember my chains) — not a bid for sympathy but a theological claim: the chains validate the gospel that caused the imprisonment. The closing grace-formula mirrors and completes the opening grace (1:2), framing the entire letter within divine gift.
